key_points: Work done depends on both magnitude and direction of force; W = F × d × cosθ, where θ is angle between force and displacement.
Positive work increases kinetic energy; negative work decreases it; zero work when displacement ⟂ force.
Kinetic energy: KE = 1/2 mv²; consider vector nature of velocity in 2D/3D motion.
Potential energy: PE = mgh; for non-uniform gravitational field, integrate force over displacement.
Elastic potential energy in springs: PE_spring = 1/2 k x²; energy stored depends on displacement squared.
Work-energy theorem: Work done by all forces = change in kinetic energy; includes non-conservative forces.
Law of conservation of energy: total mechanical energy (KE + PE) conserved in absence of non-conservative forces; energy transforms among forms.
Power: P = dW/dt for variable force; average power = total work/time.
Efficiency = (useful work output / total energy input) × 100%; consider energy lost to friction, air resistance, and heat.
Mechanical advantage, real work, and energy losses are important in practical machines.
formulas: Elastic potential energy: PE_spring = 1/2 k x²
Work-energy theorem: W_total = ΔKE = KE_final − KE_initial
Conservation of energy: KE_initial + PE_initial + W_nc = KE_final + PE_final
Efficiency (%) = (Useful work output / Total energy input) × 100%
examples: A 2 kg body accelerated from 3 m/s to 6 m/s: ΔKE = 1/2 × 2 × (6² − 3²) = 27 J → Work done by net force = 27 J
Block slides down frictional incline: PE_top = mgh, KE_bottom = PE − Work_done_by_friction
Spring with k = 200 N/m stretched 0.3 m: PE_spring = 1/2 × 200 × 0.3² = 9 J
Machine uses 800 J input, loses 150 J to friction: Efficiency = (650/800) × 100 ≈ 81.25%
Inclined plane problem: Use energy conservation, account for height, friction, and acceleration along slope
exam_tips: Always consider angle between force and displacement when calculating work.
For non-uniform forces, integrate F·dx along path to find work done.
Check for energy losses due to friction and air resistance in applied problems.
Use conservation of energy for problems involving inclined planes, pulleys, and roller coasters.
In spring and elastic problems, always square displacement in PE_spring formula.
Draw energy bar diagrams or energy flow charts for complex multi-step problems.
